title: Pick the resume bullet that survives 30 seconds

mcq.question_md: Four students describe the same project on their resumes. A recruiter skims each for a few seconds. Which bullet works best?
options: "Worked on a web development project using various modern technologies"
"Built a campus notice board — Spring Boot REST API with Postgres, React frontend, deployed on Render and Vercel"
"Hardworking team player passionate about coding and eager to learn new things"
"Completed a project as part of the final-year curriculum under faculty guidance"
mcq.explanation_md: The strong bullet names *what you built* and *the exact tech*, and "deployed" implies a clickable proof. It also hands the interviewer ready-made follow-up questions — "why Postgres?", "how did you handle CORS?" — and follow-ups you can answer are how fresher interviews are won. Option A is the tempting one because it sounds professional, but "various modern technologies" is a claim with zero evidence; the recruiter learns nothing and moves on. Option C describes a personality, not work — it belongs nowhere. Option D describes *permission* to do a project, not the project. Rule of thumb: every project bullet should answer "what, with what, and where can I see it?"
